title: Getting started with topocast
text: need fine-resolution terrain coarse climate observation. build terrain 100 m smooth surface, aggregate elevation 600 m grid stand coarse product. Precipitation observed coarse grid. coarse grid landscape 600 m, six fine cells side. Precipitation falls elevation rises steeper ground, noise terrain explain. observe coarse grid . response predictors go one SpatRaster, share grid construction. fine predictors go second raster target grid.
code: set.seed(1) fine <- rast(nrows = 120, ncols = 120,              xmin = 0, xmax = 12000, ymin = 0, ymax = 12000,              crs = "EPSG:32632") xy <- crds(fine) elev_fine <- setValues(fine, 1200 + 600 * sin(xy[, 1] / 2500) +                                400 * cos(xy[, 2] / 2000)) names(elev_fine) <- "elev" slope_fine <- terrain(elev_fine, v = "slope", unit = "degrees") names(slope_fine) <- "slope" elev_coarse  <- aggregate(elev_fine,  fact = 6, fun = "mean") slope_coarse <- aggregate(slope_fine, fact = 6, fun = "mean") prec_coarse <- 900 - 0.18 * elev_coarse + 4 * slope_coarse +   setValues(elev_coarse, rnorm(ncell(elev_coarse), 0, 20)) names(prec_coarse) <- "prec" coarse  <- c(prec_coarse, elev_coarse, slope_coarse) terrain <- c(elev_fine, slope_fine) coarse #> class       : SpatRaster #> size        : 20, 20, 3  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) #> resolution  : 600, 600  (x, y) #> extent      : 0, 12000, 0, 12000  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) #> coord. ref. : WGS 84 / UTM zone 32N (EPSG:32632) #> source(s)   : memory #> names       :       prec,        elev,     slope #> min values  : 529.303613,  203.183895,  1.281186 #> max values  : 918.058795, 2192.634046, 17.237889

title: Getting started with topocast
text: single call downscales coarse precipitation onto fine elevation. formula names response predictor; names must layers coarse, predictor must also layer onto. result 120 120 grid coarse input 20 20. Plotting two side side shows detail comes : coarse field sets broad level, elevation carries texture.  radius measured coarse cells. radius 4 means regression sees 9 9 window coarse cells, 5.4 km neighbourhood. Inside window topocast fits one intercept one slope, resamples coefficients fine grid evaluates intercept + slope * elev every fine cell.
code: fine_prec <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = coarse, onto = terrain, radius = 4) fine_prec #> class       : SpatRaster #> size        : 120, 120, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) #> resolution  : 100, 100  (x, y) #> extent      : 0, 12000, 0, 12000  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) #> coord. ref. : WGS 84 / UTM zone 32N (EPSG:32632) #> source(s)   : memory #> name        :       prec #> min value   : 523.348712 #> max value   : 891.435878 par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(2, 2, 2, 4)) plot(prec_coarse, main = "coarse precipitation (600 m)") plot(fine_prec,   main = "downscaled (100 m)")

title: Getting started with topocast
text: Naming layers adds predictors. one matched name data onto, layer order matter missing layer error rather silent mismatch. misnamed predictor stops call lists available, saves debugging session quietly wrong raster.
code: fine_prec2 <- topocast(prec ~ elev + slope, data = coarse, onto = terrain,                        radius = 4) topocast(prec ~ elev + aspect, data = coarse, onto = terrain, radius = 4) #> Error in `resolve_predictors()`: #> ! predictor `aspect` named in `formula` is in neither `data` nor `onto`. #>   data layers: prec, elev, slope #>   onto layers: elev, slope

title: Getting started with topocast
text: common case one coarse climate layer one fine elevation model, coarse predictor hand. predictor named formula absent data derived onto aggregating response grid, coarse elevation built hand. data coarse precipitation alone; elev slope aggregated terrain coarse grid aggregate, cell average default, fit. downscaled field reproduces explicit-predictor result resampling tolerance.
code: shortcut <- topocast(prec ~ elev + slope, data = prec_coarse, onto = terrain,                      radius = 4) round(max(abs(values(shortcut) - values(fine_prec2)), na.rm = TRUE), 4) #> [1] 1e-04

title: Getting started with topocast
text: reason downscale regression rather interpolation local relationship response terrain. coefficients = TRUE returns relationship grids: fitted layer, intercept, one slope per predictor, fine grid.  slope layer local precipitation lapse rate. steep, elevation explains variation precipitation; near zero, terrain carries little signal downscaled field stays close coarse input.
code: grids <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = coarse, onto = terrain, radius = 4,                   coefficients = TRUE) names(grids) #> [1] "prec"        "(Intercept)" "elev"  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(2, 2, 2, 4)) plot(grids[["prec"]], main = "downscaled precipitation") plot(grids[["elev"]], main = "local slope (mm per m)")

title: Getting started with topocast
text: Climate downscaling usually means many time steps sharing one terrain relationship. Passing stack coarse periods anomaly fits baseline carries period onto . Use type = "ratio" non-negative variables precipitation type = "additive" variables temperature. baseline climatology downscaled regression; period fine baseline scaled period’s coarse anomaly. ratio path guards zero baseline returning NA rather dividing zero.
code: jan <- prec_coarse * 0.4 jul <- prec_coarse * 1.6 months <- c(jan, jul) names(months) <- c("jan", "jul")  series <- topocast(prec ~ elev + slope, data = coarse, onto = terrain,                    radius = 4, anomaly = months, type = "ratio") nlyr(series) #> [1] 2 par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(2, 2, 2, 4)) plot(series[["jan"]], main = "January (100 m)") plot(series[["jul"]], main = "July (100 m)")

title: Getting started with topocast
text: common target set locations instead full grid: vegetation plots, weather stations, survey sites. Passing points onto evaluates fitted relationship one returns points prediction column. points carry fine predictors attributes; read elevation model. fit point coarse coefficients interpolated location applied point’s predictors, regression grid path evaluates per cell. Overlaying points downscaled field shows two agree.  call returns plain data frame coordinates predictions output = "data.frame", joining back plot table. Gridded inputs tied SpatRaster either. Raster* object raster package stars object accepted data onto, result returned class given; output requests another. class sets container result, numbers .
code: library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.14.1, GDAL 3.12.1, PROJ 9.7.1; sf_use_s2() is TRUE  plots <- st_as_sf(   data.frame(x = c(2000, 4000, 6000, 8000, 10000, 5000),              y = c(3000, 8000, 5000, 9000,  2000, 6000)),   coords = c("x", "y"), crs = "EPSG:32632")  at_terrain <- terra::extract(terrain, vect(plots)) plots$elev  <- at_terrain$elev plots$slope <- at_terrain$slope  at_plots <- topocast(prec ~ elev + slope, data = coarse, onto = plots, radius = 4) at_plots #> Simple feature collection with 6 features and 3 fields #> Geometry type: POINT #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: 2000 ymin: 2000 xmax: 10000 ymax: 9000 #> Projected CRS: WGS 84 / UTM zone 32N #>             geometry      elev     slope     prec #> 1  POINT (2000 3000) 1676.9474 14.449248 657.9065 #> 2  POINT (4000 8000) 1530.3309  8.444084 661.3340 #> 3  POINT (6000 5000) 1281.9755 12.323577 717.4466 #> 4  POINT (8000 9000) 1058.9375 17.128046 775.1305 #> 5 POINT (10000 2000)  962.6333 12.706625 775.3137 #> 6  POINT (5000 6000) 1346.0137  6.239848 683.3556 pv <- vect(plots) plot(fine_prec2, main = "downscaled precipitation, with plot predictions") plot(pv, add = TRUE, pch = 21, cex = 1.3) text(crds(pv), labels = round(at_plots$prec), pos = 3, cex = 0.8) topocast(prec ~ elev + slope, data = coarse, onto = plots, radius = 4,          output = "data.frame") #>       x    y     prec #> 1  2000 3000 657.9065 #> 2  4000 8000 661.3340 #> 3  6000 5000 717.4466 #> 4  8000 9000 775.1305 #> 5 10000 2000 775.3137 #> 6  5000 6000 683.3556

title: Getting started with topocast
text: topocast() terra workflow matrix engine, window_regression(), fits moving-window regression plain numeric matrices returns intercept slope grids. useful testing callers already hold data matrices. elevation slope recovers coefficient simulated. window spans grid local fit converges global value.
code: y <- as.matrix(prec_coarse, wide = TRUE) x <- list(as.matrix(elev_coarse, wide = TRUE),           as.matrix(slope_coarse, wide = TRUE)) fit <- window_regression(y, x, radius = 4) str(fit, max.level = 2) #> List of 2 #>  $ intercept: num [1:20, 1:20] 823 794 881 854 889 ... #>  $ slope    :List of 2 #>   ..$ : num [1:20, 1:20] -0.164 -0.154 -0.184 -0.179 -0.188 ... #>   ..$ : num [1:20, 1:20] 7.57 8.33 5.85 7.11 5.71 ... big <- window_regression(y, x, radius = 19) round(median(big$slope[[1]], na.rm = TRUE), 3)   # simulated -0.18 #> [1] -0.178

title: Getting started with topocast
text: choices shape result. Radius. window sets local relationship . small radius lets slope vary across map needs enough valid cells fit; two predictors window must hold least three cells, stable fit. large radius approaches single global regression. Start near scale expect climate-terrain relationship stationary, often couple dozen coarse cells. Resampling method. coefficient grids resampled fine grid method, defaulting "cubicspline". Cubic spline gives smooth coefficient surfaces; "bilinear" safer choice coefficients noisy want avoid overshoot. Degenerate windows. cell returns NA window fewer valid cells model needs, predictor spread (min_variance), design singular. min_cells raises valid-cell requirement bare minimum want margin. Collinear predictors. small extent, precipitation elevation can nearly collinear, makes local slope unstable even though fit succeeds. property data, defect; widen window, drop predictor, inspect slope grid (via coefficients = TRUE) noise. Deriving coarse predictor. predictor named formula layer data, aggregated onto response grid aggregate, cell average default. Set another [terra::resample()] method different aggregation suits predictor. output conserves. downscaled field regression surface evaluated fine predictors. Averaging back coarse grid reproduce coarse input exactly, coarse residual carried. anomaly path restores fidelity coarse values time: period’s coarse anomaly carried verbatim onto fine baseline. Coordinate systems. data onto share coordinate reference system. Two systems EPSG code treated equal even WKT strings differ, common cross-source lon/lat data DEM climate product different providers. genuine mismatch error names systems. See vignette("--works", package = "topocast") summed-area-table method behind constant per-cell cost.

title: How moving-window downscaling works
text: global regression coarse response yy kk predictors x1,…,xkx_1, \dots, x_k assumes one relationship whole map. Terrain-climate relationships stable: lapse rate windward slope differs one rain shadow. topocast instead fits separate ordinary least squares regression square window around every coarse cell. cell row rr, column cc, window radius ρ\rho holds cells within ρ\rho rows columns, fit solves minβ∑(,j)∈Wrc(yij−β0−∑m=1kβmxm,ij)2. \min_{\beta} \sum_{(,j) \W_{rc}} \Big( y_{ij} - \beta_0 - \sum_{m=1}^{k} \beta_m \, x_{m,ij} \Big)^2 . result grid intercepts β̂0(r,c)\hat\beta_0(r,c) grid slopes β̂m(r,c)\hat\beta_m(r,c) predictor, coarse resolution. coefficient grids locally varying relationship response terrain, topocast(..., coefficients = TRUE) returns fine grid alongside fitted layer. downscaling step resamples coefficient grid fine resolution evaluates regression fine predictors: ŷ(f)=β̂0(f)+∑m=1kβ̂m(f)xm(f), \hat y(f) = \hat\beta_0(f) + \sum_{m=1}^{k} \hat\beta_m(f)\, x_m(f), ff indexes fine cells coefficients resampled coarse grids. fine-scale structure output comes entirely fine predictors; coefficients carry relationship.

title: How moving-window downscaling works
text: naive moving window re-sums every cell neighbourhood every centre, cost grows window area. radius 20 1,600 cells touched per output cell. topocast avoids summed-area tables, also called integral images. normal equations window fit Aβ=bA \beta = b, AA holds within-window sums ∑1\sum 1, ∑xm\sum x_m, ∑xmxm′\sum x_m x_{m'}, bb holds ∑y\sum y ∑xmy\sum x_m y. sum fixed quantity window. grid gg, summed-area table SS stores sum rectangle top-left corner cell: S(r,c)=∑≤r,j≤cgij. S(r,c) = \sum_{\le r,\, j \le c} g_{ij}. SS built single pass, sum rectangular window four lookups: ∑(,j)∈Wgij=S(r1,c1)−S(r0−1,c1)−S(r1,c0−1)+S(r0−1,c0−1). \sum_{(,j)\W} g_{ij} = S(r_1,c_1) - S(r_0-1,c_1) - S(r_1,c_0-1) + S(r_0-1,c_0-1). topocast builds one table cell count, one ∑y\sum y, one per predictor ∑xm\sum x_m ∑xmy\sum x_m y, one per predictor pair ∑xmxm′\sum x_m x_{m'}. Every window assembles AA bb four lookups , solves (k+1)×(k+1)(k{+}1) \times (k{+}1) system, stores coefficients. total cost proportional number cells small per-cell solve, dependence radius. radius changes smoothness slope grid runtime.
code: set.seed(1) g <- rast(nrows = 80, ncols = 80, xmin = 0, xmax = 8000, ymin = 0, ymax = 8000,           crs = "EPSG:32632") elev <- setValues(g, 1000 + 500 * sin(crds(g)[, 1] / 1500)) prec <- 800 - 0.15 * elev + setValues(g, rnorm(ncell(g), 0, 15))  y <- as.matrix(prec, wide = TRUE) x <- as.matrix(elev, wide = TRUE) small <- window_regression(y, x, radius = 3) large <- window_regression(y, x, radius = 15)  par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(2, 2, 2, 4)) plot(setValues(elev, as.vector(t(small$slope[[1]]))), main = "slope, radius 3") plot(setValues(elev, as.vector(t(large$slope[[1]]))), main = "slope, radius 15")

title: How moving-window downscaling works
text: Global centring. accumulation response predictor centred global mean valid cells. Centring keeps entries AA small even elevation runs thousands, conditions solve. intercept mapped back raw response predictor scale output, returned coefficients original units. Complete-case masking. cell contributes window response every predictor finite. separate count table tracks many valid cells fall window, invalid cells set zero centred copies drop sums without distorting . Degenerate windows. cell returned NA three cases: window holds fewer valid cells model needs (k + 1, raised min_cells); predictor within-window variance min_variance, slope identified; assembled system singular. guards keep flat data-starved neighbourhood producing meaningless coefficient.

title: How moving-window downscaling works
text: climatology one field. time series shares terrain relationship across many periods, refitting regression period wasteful let spatial pattern drift steps. topocast instead downscales baseline carries period’s coarse anomaly onto fine baseline. period coarse value vv, coarse baseline bb, fine baseline ŷb\hat y_b, ratio path returns ŷ=ŷb⋅vb, \hat y = \hat y_b \cdot \frac{v}{b}, additive path returns ŷ=ŷb+(v−b)\hat y = \hat y_b + (v - b). ratio suits non-negative variables precipitation; additive form suits variables temperature difference natural anomaly. carry coarse anomaly verbatim, downscaled series tracks coarse values time even though baseline regression surface. zero missing baseline ratio path returns NA rather dividing zero.

title: How moving-window downscaling works

Averaging fine field back coarse grid reproduce coarse input, coarse residual, part yy regression explain, discarded. smoothed climate product usually acceptable, anomaly path restores fidelity coarse values time-varying part. slopes applied full fine-predictor range. fine elevation reaches values coarse window never saw, regression extrapolates linearly. fine output trustworthy linear relationship extended range, reason keep predictors stay roughly linear response inspect result terrain extreme.

what: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data
title: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data
text: Moving-window regression downscaling raster data. topocast rewrites coarse raster resolution finer one learning coarse variable tracks terrain, locally, applying relationship fine-resolution predictors. coarse precipitation grid plus digital elevation model becomes precipitation field resolution elevation model. relationship fit per neighbourhood moving-window regression whose cost grow window size.

title: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data
code: library(topocast) library(terra)  coarse  <- c(prec_1km, elev_1km)      # response + predictors, one coarse grid names(coarse) <- c("prec", "elev") terrain <- elev_100m                   # the predictor at the target resolution names(terrain) <- "elev"  fine <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = coarse, onto = terrain, radius = 15)

title: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data
text: climate variable often tracks elevation roughly linear relationship, high-resolution elevation model can carry variable finer grid regression. step behind high-resolution climate surfaces CHELSA (Karger et al. 2017). Two things limit usual implementations: relationship fit globally single predictor, moving-window fit costs window grows. topocast fits regression locally window around every cell, takes number named terrain predictors, uses summed-area tables per-cell cost four lookups regardless radius. relationship formula layer names, matches predictors coarse fine grids turns misnamed layer error rather silently wrong result.

title: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data
text: Formula interface. prec ~ elev + slope + twi names response predictors; names match layers data onto. terrain predictors. Elevation, slope, aspect, topographic wetness, aligned covariate, fit jointly. Cost independent window size. Summed-area tables reduce window fit four lookups per sufficient statistic, radius 30 costs radius 3. Time series one call. Pass stack coarse periods anomaly downscale baseline carry period onto , ratio additive. terra-free engine. window_regression() exposes matrix kernel testing callers hold data matrices.

title: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data
Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data
text: Several predictors named formula matched name: time series shares one terrain relationship across periods. Pass periods anomaly (type = "ratio" precipitation, type = "additive" temperature):
code: coarse  <- c(prec_1km, elev_1km, twi_1km, slope_1km) names(coarse)  <- c("prec", "elev", "twi", "slope") terrain <- c(elev_100m, twi_100m, slope_100m) names(terrain) <- c("elev", "twi", "slope")  fine <- topocast(prec ~ elev + twi + slope, data = coarse, onto = terrain,                  radius = 15) series <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = coarse, onto = terrain, radius = 15,                    anomaly = prec_monthly_1km, type = "ratio")

what: topocast: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data — topocast-package
title: topocast: Moving-Window Regression Downscaling of Raster Data — topocast-package
text: Downscales coarse-resolution raster data finer grid fitting local linear regressions response, climate variable, one fine-resolution predictors, elevation terrain indices, within moving window. Regression coefficients estimated every cell using summed-area tables, cost independent window size, resampled target resolution applied fine-resolution predictors. Multiplicative additive anomaly application downscale time series relative baseline climatology, following regression--elevation approach used high-resolution climate surfaces (Karger et al. 2017) doi:10.1038/sdata.2017.122 .

title: Downscale a coarse raster onto fine terrain by moving-window regression — topocast
code: topocast(   formula,   data,   onto,   radius,   aggregate = "average",   coefficients = FALSE,   anomaly = NULL,   baseline = NULL,   type = c("ratio", "additive"),   method = "cubicspline",   output = NULL,   min_cells = 0L,   min_variance = 1e-08 )

title: Downscale a coarse raster onto fine terrain by moving-window regression — topocast
text: formula two-sided formula bare layer names, prec ~ elev + slope. left-hand side names coarse response layer data; right-hand side names predictor layers. data gridded coarse input holding response layer , optionally, predictor layers named formula: SpatRaster, Raster* (raster), stars object. predictor data derived onto. onto target. gridded SpatRaster, Raster*, stars object whose grid defines output, holding every predictor layer named formula; sf/SpatVector points carrying predictors attributes, case fit evaluated points. point onto every predictor must layer data (derive--onto shortcut needs grid). radius Integer window radius coarse cells; window square side 2 * radius + 1. aggregate Resampling method used derive coarse predictor onto already layer data, passed terra::resample(). Default "average". coefficients TRUE, return fitted layer together (Intercept) per-predictor slope grids onto grid. supported anomaly. Default FALSE. anomaly Optional multi-layer SpatRaster coarse grid; layer one period downscale relative baseline. supplied, result one layer (column) per period. baseline Optional single-layer SpatRaster, coarse response baseline anomaly taken relative . Defaults response layer data. Ignored anomaly NULL. type "ratio" (multiplicative) "additive"; used anomaly. method Resampling method coefficient grids, passed terra::resample(). Default "cubicspline". output Optional output class, one "terra", "raster", "stars" (grid targets) "terra", "sf", "spatvector", "data.frame" (point targets). Default NULL returns result class onto. min_cells, min_variance Passed window_regression().

title: Downscale a coarse raster onto fine terrain by moving-window regression — topocast
text: relationship given formula whose names refer layers data (coarse grid) onto (target grid). response coarse predictors layers single data raster, guaranteed share grid; formula names match predictors data onto, missing mis-ordered layer error rather silently wrong result. bare layer names combined + supported, prec ~ elev + slope. Transformations (log(elev)), interactions (elev:slope), . rejected; create derived layer first. common case one coarse response fine predictor digital elevation model, coarse predictor hand. predictor named formula absent data derived aggregating onto layer response grid aggregate, topocast(prec ~ elev, data = prec_coarse, onto = dem_fine, radius = 15) works directly coarse climate layer fine DEM. time series, supply anomaly: stack coarse periods. baseline relationship fit period's coarse anomaly, relative baseline, carried onto fine baseline. Use type = "ratio" non-negative variables precipitation type = "additive" variables temperature. data onto may common spatial classes. gridded input accepted SpatRaster, Raster* object (raster), stars object. target onto may instead set points sf SpatVector object, case fitted relationship evaluated point prediction column returned; points must carry fine predictor values attributes. default result returned class onto; set output request another.

title: Downscale a coarse raster onto fine terrain by moving-window regression — topocast
code: library(terra) set.seed(1) coarse <- rast(nrows = 20, ncols = 20, xmin = 0, xmax = 20, ymin = 0, ymax = 20,                crs = "EPSG:32632") elevation <- setValues(coarse, runif(ncell(coarse), 0, 2000)) precipitation <- 800 - 0.1 * elevation data <- c(precipitation, elevation) names(data) <- c("prec", "elev") terrain <- disagg(elevation, fact = 4, method = "bilinear") names(terrain) <- "elev"  # spatial downscale fine <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = data, onto = terrain, radius = 4)  # one-DEM shortcut: the coarse predictor is derived from the fine DEM fine2 <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = data[["prec"]], onto = terrain, radius = 4)  # return the local coefficient grids coef_grids <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = data, onto = terrain, radius = 4,                        coefficients = TRUE)  # time series: supply the periods months <- precipitation * c(0.8, 1.2) names(months) <- c("jan", "feb") series <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = data, onto = terrain, radius = 4,                    anomaly = months, type = "ratio")  # predict at point locations: onto is sf points carrying the predictor if (requireNamespace("sf", quietly = TRUE)) {   plots <- sf::st_as_sf(     data.frame(x = c(5, 10, 15), y = c(5, 10, 15), elev = c(500, 1000, 1500)),     coords = c("x", "y"), crs = "EPSG:32632")   at_plots <- topocast(prec ~ elev, data = data, onto = plots, radius = 4) }

text: Fits, every cell, linear regression response one predictors square moving window, returns full-resolution intercept slope grids. fit uses summed-area tables, cost grow window size. Cells excluded window response predictor non-finite; cell returned NA window holds fewer valid cells model needs predictor spread.

title: Moving-window linear regression over raster matrices — window_regression
Moving-window linear regression over raster matrices — window_regression
text: y Numeric matrix, response coarse grid. x Numeric matrix, list numeric matrices, predictor(s) grid y. radius Integer window radius cells; window square side 2 * radius + 1. min_cells Integer, additional valid cells required window beyond k + 1 model terms (k predictors plus intercept). Default 0. min_variance Numeric, minimum within-window variance predictor must cell fit. Default 1e-8.

title: Moving-window linear regression over raster matrices — window_regression

title: Moving-window linear regression over raster matrices — window_regression
code: set.seed(1) elevation <- matrix(runif(100, 0, 1000), 10, 10) climate <- 50 + 0.01 * elevation fit <- window_regression(climate, elevation, radius = 3) str(fit)
